quiz_questions.question_text: An outlier is a data value that:

options.text: Falls at the median
Is the most common value
Equals the mean
Is significantly different from the other values in the data set

quiz_questions.explanation: An outlier is an observation that lies an abnormal distance from other values in the data set, either extremely high or extremely low.

quiz_questions.question_text: Using the 1.5 x IQR rule, a value is an outlier if it is:

options.text: Below Q1 - 1.5(IQR) or above Q3 + 1.5(IQR)
Below the mean
The highest value
Equal to the median

quiz_questions.explanation: The 1.5 x IQR rule defines outlier boundaries (fences) at Q1 - 1.5(IQR) and Q3 + 1.5(IQR). Any data point beyond these fences is classified as an outlier.

quiz_questions.question_text: Adding a high outlier to a data set will:

options.text: Not change any statistics
Decrease both the mean and median
Only change the mode
Increase the mean but barely change the median

quiz_questions.explanation: A high outlier pulls the mean upward because it is included in the sum, but the median barely changes because it only depends on the middle position.

quiz_questions.question_text: Which measure of spread is most affected by outliers?

options.text: Neither is affected
Both are equally affected
IQR
Range

quiz_questions.explanation: The range depends directly on the minimum and maximum values, so an outlier at either extreme dramatically changes the range. The IQR is resistant to outliers.

quiz_questions.question_text: For the data {2, 3, 4, 5, 100}, the mean is 22.8 and the median is 4. Which better represents the typical value?

options.text: Both are equally good
The mean (22.8)
The median (4)
Neither represents the data

quiz_questions.explanation: The value 100 is an outlier that inflates the mean to 22.8, far above most values. The median of 4 is much closer to the other four values and is more representative.

quiz_questions.question_text: Removing an outlier from a data set will typically:

options.text: Make the mean and median further apart
Only change the mode
Not change any statistics
Bring the mean closer to the median

quiz_questions.explanation: Outliers pull the mean away from the center. Removing an outlier allows the mean to move back toward the median, making the two measures more similar.

quiz_questions.question_text: Standard deviation is affected by outliers because:

options.text: It equals the range
It measures distances from the mean, and outliers are far from the mean
It only uses the middle values
It ignores extreme values

quiz_questions.explanation: Standard deviation is based on squared deviations from the mean. An outlier creates a very large squared deviation, inflating the standard deviation.

quiz_questions.question_text: An outlier on the low end of a data set will:

options.text: Pull the mean up
Only change the median
Not change the mean
Pull the mean down

quiz_questions.explanation: A low outlier decreases the sum of the data, pulling the mean downward, away from the center of the bulk of the data.

quiz_questions.question_text: If Q1 = 20, Q3 = 40, what is the upper fence for outliers?
Why might an outlier occur in a data set?

options.text: Only due to data entry errors
Outliers never occur naturally
Only when the sample size is small
Measurement error, data entry error, or a genuinely extreme observation

quiz_questions.explanation: Outliers can result from mistakes (typos, instrument errors) or from genuinely extreme observations that are part of the natural variation in the data.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is an outlier?

options.text: The average of the data
The most frequent value
A data value that is significantly higher or lower than the rest of the data in a set
The range of the data

quiz_questions.explanation: An outlier is an extreme value that lies far from the majority of the data. It can occur on either the high or low end of the distribution.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'resistant' mean in the context of statistics?

options.text: A statistic that only applies to large data sets
A statistic that changes dramatically with any new data
A statistic that is not much affected by extreme values or outliers
A statistic that is always zero

quiz_questions.explanation: A resistant statistic is one that is not heavily influenced by outliers or extreme values. The median and IQR are resistant; the mean and standard deviation are not.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the '1.5 x IQR rule'?

options.text: A rule that the IQR must be at least 1.5
A mathematical rule that classifies any value below Q1 - 1.5(IQR) or above Q3 + 1.5(IQR) as an outlier
A method to calculate the mean
A rule about standard deviation

quiz_questions.explanation: The 1.5 x IQR rule establishes fences at Q1 - 1.5(IQR) and Q3 + 1.5(IQR). Data points beyond these fences are formally classified as outliers.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'robust' mean when describing a statistic?

options.text: The statistic changes with every new data point
The statistic remains reliable and is not significantly influenced by outliers or non-normal data
The statistic is always positive
The statistic is accurate only for large data sets

quiz_questions.explanation: A robust statistic maintains its reliability even when the data contains outliers or deviates from assumptions. The median is considered robust; the mean is not.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'fence' in outlier detection?

options.text: The minimum and maximum of the data set
The calculated boundary values beyond which data points are considered outliers
The mean of the data set
The whiskers of a box plot

quiz_questions.explanation: Fences are the boundary values calculated using the 1.5 x IQR rule: lower fence = Q1 - 1.5(IQR) and upper fence = Q3 + 1.5(IQR).

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'sensitivity to outliers' mean?

options.text: A statistic that never changes
A statistic that detects outliers
A statistic that equals the outlier value
A statistic that changes significantly when outliers are present

quiz_questions.explanation: Sensitivity to outliers means the statistic's value shifts substantially when extreme data points are present. The mean and standard deviation are sensitive to outliers.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'influence' mean when discussing an outlier's effect?

options.text: Whether the outlier is positive or negative
The power of the outlier to change or distort summary statistics like the mean or standard deviation
The distance between Q1 and Q3
The number of outliers in the data set

quiz_questions.explanation: An outlier's influence refers to how much it changes the value of a summary statistic. A single extreme outlier can have a large influence on the mean and standard deviation.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is the 'effect of an outlier on the range'?

options.text: An outlier decreases the range
An outlier dramatically increases the range because the range depends on the minimum and maximum
An outlier only affects the range if it is negative
An outlier has no effect on the range

quiz_questions.explanation: Since the range is max minus min, any outlier at the high or low end directly changes the range, often making it much larger than it would be without the outlier.

quiz_questions.question_text: What does 'non-resistant' mean for a statistic?

options.text: The statistic is always zero
The statistic is always negative
The statistic cannot be calculated
The statistic is heavily influenced by outliers or extreme values

quiz_questions.explanation: A non-resistant statistic is one that changes significantly when outliers are present. The mean, range, and standard deviation are non-resistant measures.

quiz_questions.question_text: What is a 'deviation from the mean'?

options.text: The difference between a data value and the mean of the data set
The difference between Q1 and Q3
The range of the data set
The mode of the data set

quiz_questions.explanation: A deviation from the mean is calculated as (data value - mean). Outliers have very large deviations, which inflate measures like variance and standard deviation.

quiz_questions.question_text: A small business records daily revenue: $200, $220, $210, $230, $215, $5000. The $5000 day was due to a one-time bulk order. How does this outlier affect the mean daily revenue compared to the median?

options.text: The median is higher than the mean
Neither is affected by the outlier
Both the mean and median are about $220
The mean is about $1012.50 while the median is about $217.50, so the mean greatly overestimates typical daily revenue

quiz_questions.explanation: The sum is 6075, giving a mean of 6075/6 = $1012.50. The median of the sorted data is (215+220)/2 = $217.50. The $5000 outlier makes the mean nearly five times the typical daily revenue.

quiz_questions.question_text: A teacher records quiz scores: 88, 90, 85, 92, 87, 91, 15. The 15 was from a student who was absent and took a makeup quiz. Should the teacher include or exclude this outlier when reporting the class average?

options.text: Exclude it or report the median, because the outlier misrepresents the typical class performance
It does not matter
Include it, because all data must always be included
Replace it with the mean

quiz_questions.explanation: The 15 is an outlier due to special circumstances. Including it drops the mean from about 88.8 to 78.3, misrepresenting the class. The teacher should note the outlier and consider using the median or reporting both with and without the outlier.

quiz_questions.question_text: A real estate agent has home prices: $180K, $190K, $200K, $195K, $185K, $1.2M. What is the range with and without the $1.2M outlier?

options.text: Both ranges are $20K
With outlier: $1,020K; Without outlier: $1,000K
With outlier: $200K; Without outlier: $20K
With outlier: $1,020K; Without outlier: $20K

quiz_questions.explanation: With the outlier: $1,200K - $180K = $1,020K. Without: $200K - $180K = $20K. The single outlier multiplied the range by over 50 times.

quiz_questions.question_text: A marathon organizer records finish times (in minutes): 240, 245, 250, 255, 260, 265, 480. The 480-minute time was a walker who got lost. If Q1 = 242.5, Q3 = 262.5, and IQR = 20, is 480 an outlier by the 1.5 x IQR rule?

options.text: No, because it is the maximum value
Cannot be determined
No, because it is within 2 standard deviations

quiz_questions.explanation: Upper fence = Q3 + 1.5(IQR) = 262.5 + 1.5(20) = 262.5 + 30 = 292.5. Since 480 > 292.5, the 480-minute time is formally classified as an outlier.

quiz_questions.question_text: A scientist measures reaction times (in ms) for 10 subjects: 250, 260, 255, 270, 265, 258, 262, 268, 255, 900. The 900 ms was a distracted subject. How does removing this outlier affect the standard deviation?

options.text: The standard deviation increases
The standard deviation decreases substantially because the most extreme value is removed
The standard deviation becomes zero
The standard deviation stays the same

quiz_questions.explanation: The 900 ms value creates a very large squared deviation from the mean, inflating the standard deviation. Removing it brings the SD down dramatically because the remaining values are tightly clustered around 255-270.

quiz_questions.question_text: An employer reviews salaries: $40K, $42K, $45K, $43K, $44K, $300K (CEO). The HR department wants to report a 'typical' salary. Which approach is most appropriate?

options.text: Report the median of $43.5K, since the CEO salary is an outlier that skews the mean
Report the range of $260K
Report the mean of $85.7K
Report the mode

quiz_questions.explanation: The CEO's $300K salary is an outlier that inflates the mean to $85.7K, far above any non-CEO salary. The median of $43.5K accurately represents what a typical employee earns.

quiz_questions.question_text: A basketball player scores the following points in 8 games: 18, 22, 20, 19, 21, 23, 20, 2. The 2-point game was due to an early injury. How do the mean and median change if that game is excluded?

options.text: Both the mean and median stay the same
Both decrease
The mean stays the same but the median increases
The mean increases from 18.1 to 20.4, and the median increases from 20 to 20

quiz_questions.explanation: With all 8 games: mean = 145/8 = 18.1, median = 20. Without the 2-point game: mean = 143/7 = 20.4, median = 20. The mean increases significantly while the median is unaffected.

quiz_questions.question_text: A car dealer tracks days to sell each car: 5, 8, 10, 7, 12, 9, 6, 120. The 120-day car was a rare vintage model. How does this outlier affect the dealer's reported average selling time?

options.text: It only changes the mode
It raises the mean from about 8.1 days to about 22.1 days, making sales appear much slower than typical
It lowers the mean
It has no effect on any statistics

quiz_questions.explanation: Without the outlier: mean = 57/7 = 8.1 days. With the outlier: mean = 177/8 = 22.1 days. The single outlier nearly triples the reported average, misrepresenting typical sales.

quiz_questions.question_text: A researcher collects test scores: 70, 72, 74, 76, 78, 80, 82, 84, 86, 88. She accidentally enters 880 instead of 88. How does this error affect the mean?

options.text: The mean increases from 79 to 86.6
The mean stays at 79
The mean increases from 79 to 158.2
The mean decreases from 79 to 70

quiz_questions.explanation: Correct sum = 790, mean = 79. Erroneous sum = 790 - 88 + 880 = 1582, mean = 158.2. A single data entry error (outlier) changed the mean from 79 to 158.2, more than doubling it.

quiz_questions.question_text: A nutritionist tracks daily water intake (in cups) for a patient: 8, 7, 8, 9, 7, 8, 8, 1. The 1-cup day was when the patient was ill. What effect does this low outlier have on the IQR compared to the range?

options.text: Both the IQR and range are equally affected
Neither statistic is affected
The IQR is dramatically affected but the range is not
The range is dramatically increased by the outlier (range = 8), but the IQR is barely affected because Q1 and Q3 are based on the middle portion

quiz_questions.explanation: The range is 9 - 1 = 8, heavily influenced by the outlier. The IQR depends on Q1 and Q3, which are based on the middle 50% of the data and are barely changed by the single extreme value.
